title: Calligraphy warm-up exercises
subtitle: The strokes and drills that prepare the hand before writing a calligraphy session
excerpt: Broad-edge and pointed-pen calligraphy both benefit from five minutes of warm-up strokes before writing text. These drills establish consistent nib angle, ink flow, and muscle memory, so the first letters of the session are as clean as the last.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on practice methods. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingillumina00john. Johnston recommends beginning each practice session with a set of foundational strokes to establish the hand before working on letterforms.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pen stroke that moves away from the writer, against the natural pulling direction. Push strokes are hairlines in broad-edge calligraphy; they require a light touch to avoid catching the nib on the paper.
A pen stroke that moves toward the writer, with gravity and the natural direction of hand movement. Pull strokes produce the weighted downstrokes in both broad-edge and pointed-pen scripts.

Maintaining the same angle between the nib and the writing line throughout a session. Broad-edge scripts specify the nib angle in degrees from the baseline; changing angle during writing changes stroke widths and the character of the letterforms.

content.text: Cold hand, inconsistent ink, uncertain nib angle: these are the three main reasons why the first five minutes of a calligraphy session are often the worst. Warm-up exercises address all three systematically. They are not a ritual or a delay: they are what prepares a

items.name: Tracing paper (as hand guard under writing hand)

content.text: Charge the nib and draw five straight downstrokes from x-height to baseline, holding the nib at the target angle (30 degrees for Foundational; 45 degrees for Italic or Fraktur). Space them evenly. Look for consistent width: all five should be the same weight.
Draw five branching strokes: start at the baseline, pull upward to x-height, and arc right to end at the hairline. This is the arch preparation for letters like n, m, and h.

Draw five ovals without lifting the pen. The oval is anticlockwise: start at 1 o'clock, arc left and downward to the base, continue right and upward, and close at 1 o'clock. If the closing point is above or below the starting point, the oval is not closed and the letterforms will be open.
Write the letter n five times. After the warm-up strokes, the first n should already show more consistent weight and angle than a cold-start n.
Draw ten oval shapes (anticlockwise) with no pressure, keeping the line a hairline throughout. These establish a consistent stroke path before pressure is added.

Repeat the oval exercise, this time applying pressure on the left downstroke and releasing to a hairline on the upstroke. The shade should build from the top of the left curve to the base, then release smoothly.
Draw ten underturn strokes: pull from x-height to baseline with a shaded downstroke, then turn right and
upward as a hairline to x-height. This is the basic Copperplate minuscule stroke used in u, n, and m.

Once the warm-up strokes are complete, rule the guide lines for the work session and begin writing. The warm-up practice sheet does not need to be saved; it is a functional tool, not a finished piece. Some calligraphers keep a practice journal for warm-up sheets to track improvement across sessions, but the choice is entirely practical.
